title: Digital technique puts rendered fabric in the best light
abstract: Realistic fabric rendering is still a significant challenge due to their complex structures and varying fiber properties. We present a new fabric shading technique, which models both reflection and transmission using a hybrid of ray and wave optics methods, grounded in simulation data. We target fabrics woven from yarns, each formed by twisting together one or more plies, which further contain twisted fibers. Our model is based on simulations that predict the scattering of a narrow Gaussian beam by a single ply. Comparing results from full-wave simulations and path tracing, we found that ray optics can accurately simulate the average far field scattering from an ensemble of plies, but not the variation among individual ply instances, and ray tracing overlooks important diffraction effects. Following these observations, our model is built from ray simulations performed for many ply instances, with simulation data fitted by Gaussian mixtures to be used during rendering. Wave simulations are used to calibrate noise functions that account for instance-to-instance variation, and an aperture diffraction model is used to handle light passing between plies and yarns. The result is a hybrid model capable of producing realistic appearance and highlight structure in fabrics, while capturing spatial break-ups and irregularities and simulating the subtle color shifts and blurriness that occur in transmission. We validate our results by comparing rendered images with photographs, demonstrating the effectiveness of our approach in achieving realistic cloth rendering.
introduction: In recent years, significant progress has been made in fabric render-ing, particularly through ply-based models [Zhu et al. 2024, 2023a]. These models have substantially improved realism by relying onphysical principles and measurable properties, rather than on em-pirical methods [Estevez and Kulla 2017; Zeltner et al . 2022] thatmatch appearance purely through observation.Despite these advancements, faithfully capturing the appearanceof fabrics remains challenging. Modeling both reflection and trans-mission accurately is difficult, and characteristic irregularities ofreal materials are often inadequately addressed. Additionally, manycurrent models rely on idealized assumptions—for instance, fibersare assumed to be perfectly circular and lack natural variation, andwave optics effects are ignored—which do not reflect the complexityof real-world fabrics.We propose a ply model derived from first principles, combiningthe realism of wave optics with the speed of ray optics.  Our ply scattering model is based on multi-fiber full-wave simulations andpath tracing, where both simulation methods account for the true,generalized shapes of fibers. Comparisons between wave and raysimulations show that ray optics can accurately compute the aver-age far field scattering from a large collection of plies, but not thevariation among individual ply instances. Thus, we model averageply scattering by fitting ray simulation results to a small set of Gauss-ian lobes, achieving a lightweight representation. Moreover, sincethe spatially varying and sometimes glinty appearance of fabricsarises from instance-to-instance differences in ply structures, wederive noise data from a limited set of wave simulations and usethem to augment our average ply scattering model.Moreover, our model handles multi-bounce interactions betweenmultiple plies and in double-yarn configurations, resulting in a new,more diffuse transmission model that captures complex scatteringand absorption effects across ply layers. Additionally, we introducewave optics based diffraction lobes to more realistically model trans-mission through gaps and around yarn edges. Together, these com-ponents enable a robust and accurate approach to fabric rendering,contributing to the advancement of appearance modeling.
rag_generated_news_article:
Headline:  
Advancing Cloth Rendering: Hybrid Wave-Ray Optics Bridges Multi-Scale Micro-Appearance With Physically Grounded Fabric Simulation

News Article:  
In the trajectory established by the 2019 IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics’ multi-scale hybrid micro-appearance modeling and real-time rendering of thin fabrics, the technical pursuit of digital realism in cloth appearance remains a principal challenge. That seminal work foregrounded the need to simulate reflection and transmission at multiple scales by combining physical accuracy with computational efficiency, a requirement tightly coupled to the nuanced light transport and fabric geometry that pervades virtual textile rendering. Building on this lineage, new research delivers a hybrid method—anchored in first-principles simulations—that melds wave optics and ray optics for fabric rendering, targeting the crucial deficits inherent in current models exposed by both the 2019 study and subsequent technical evaluations.

At the core of the latest methodology is a ply-centric view of fabric composition, modeling cloth as assemblies of yarns formed from twisted plies, themselves made up of multiple fibers. Prior techniques frequently relied on idealized or empirically-tuned parameters, often assuming perfectly circular fibers and neglecting the stochastic, non-uniform nature of real-world textiles. The proposed approach rejects this simplification: it leverages simulations that account for generalized, observed fiber geometries and their inherent variances. Specifically, the pipeline commences with full-wave simulations to precisely model the scattering of a narrow Gaussian beam by a single ply, directly capturing the complex physics of both reflection and transmission through multiple structural scales.

To reconcile computational tractability with physical fidelity—a technical imperative echoed by the foundational 2019 paper’s hybridization strategy—the method combines path tracing with wave and ray optics. Through systematic comparison, it was shown that while ray optics is adequate for approximating the average far-field scattering from ensembles of plies, it fails to encode the unique variability between individual ply configurations, and importantly, neglects crucial diffraction phenomena. Consequently, the rendering model couples average ply scattering (derived by fitting ray simulation results to Gaussian mixtures) with statistically-driven noise functions calibrated from wave optics simulations. This dual mechanism ensures that image outputs encapsulate both the representative mean behavior of the fabric and the particularized structural variances that produce naturalistic glint and break-up effects, as verified by side-by-side rendered image and photographic comparisons.

Transmission—an aspect that remains undercharacterized in several precedents—receives a thorough treatment via a new multi-bounce interaction model. This model extends to both single and double-yarn configurations, simulating complex scattering and absorption across multiple ply layers. Notably, a wave optics-based aperture diffraction model is introduced to account for light propagation through inter-ply gaps and edge effects at yarn boundaries. Such multi-modal transmission simulation, including resultant diffuse spread and subtle chromatic shifts, directly mitigates the shortcomings of prior approaches referenced in the anchor event, where both the empirical and geometric complexity of fabrics could not fully capture the interplay of light across fiber substructures.

The presented hybrid framework is robustly validated: rendered images faithfully align with reference photographs, and the method's capacity to simulate spatially varying and glinty appearances—direct outcomes of ply-specific structural noise—demonstrates the necessity of moving beyond ray-based models alone. The nuanced hybridization of wave and ray optics, underpinned by a data-driven mixture fit and physically-based noise calibration, embodies the multi-scale ethos advocated by earlier research. It also operationalizes a pathway for real-time or near-realistic rendering of woven textiles while maintaining adherence to measured optical and geometric properties rather than parametric or purely observational tuning.

For researchers and practitioners in computer graphics and materials simulation, this work underscores a directional advance: progress in fabric rendering now correlates with the depth of physically grounded modeling at the ply and fiber levels, as well as the successful integration of high-fidelity wave phenomena with scalable ray-based estimators. The synthesis of these modalities both validates and extends the principles articulated in the 2019 multi-scale hybrid micro-appearance modeling, affirming the future of digital textile appearance lies at the intersection of physical simulation, microgeometry analysis, and hybrid algorithmics.

Readers seeking a deeper understanding of the model’s technical implementation, validation results, and comparative analysis are encouraged to access the full research publication at the ACM Digital Library: https://dl.acm.org/doi/epdf/10.1145/3763359. Continued investigation in this domain is critical for advancing physically accurate and computationally tractable digital representations of complex materials.

title: Rosetta stone for database inputs reveals serious security issue
abstract:
We introduce the first method for translating text embeddings from one vector space to another without any paired data, encoders, or predefined sets of matches. Our unsupervised approach translates any embedding to and from a universal latent representation (i.e., a universal semantic structure conjectured by the Platonic Representation Hypothesis). Our translations achieve high cosine similarity across model pairs with different architectures, parameter counts, and training datasets. The ability to translate unknown embeddings into a different space while preserving their geometry has serious implications for security. An adversary with access to a database of only embedding vectors can extract sensitive information about underlying documents, sufficient for classification and attribute inference.

Figure 1: Left: input embeddings from different model families (T5-based GTR [46] and BERT-based GTE [32]) are fundamentally incomparable. Right: given unpaired embedding samples from different models on different texts, our model learns a latent representation where they are closely aligned.
introduction:
Introduction

Text embeddings are the backbone of modern NLP, powering tasks like retrieval, RAG, classification, and clustering. There are many embedding models trained on different datasets, data shufflings, and initializations. An embedding of a text encodes its semantics: a good model maps texts with similar semantics to vectors close to each other in the embedding space. Since semantics is a property of text, different embeddings of the same text should encode the same semantics. In practice, however, different models encode texts into completely different and incompatible vector spaces.

The Platonic Representation Hypothesis [18] conjectures that all image models of sufficient size converge to the same latent representation. We propose a stronger, constructive version of this hypothesis for text models: the universal latent structure of text representations can be learned and, furthermore, harnessed to translate representations from one space to another without any paired data or encoders.

Subject: Enron Bashing on FrontlineBody:Karen, please call me when you receive this email. Thank you. Rick

Figure 2: Given only a vector database from an unknown model, vec2vec translates the database into the space of a known model using latent structure alone. Converted embeddings reveal sensitive information about the original documents, such as the topic of an email (pictured, real example).

In this work, we show that the Strong Platonic Representation Hypothesis holds in practice. Given unpaired examples of embeddings from two models with different architectures and training data, our method learns a latent representation in which the embeddings are almost identical (Figure 1).

We draw inspiration from research on aligning word embeddings across languages [61, 10, 15, 9] and unsupervised image translation [35, 68]. Our vec2vec method uses adversarial losses and cycle consistency to learn to encode embeddings into a shared latent space and decode with minimal loss. This makes unsupervised translation possible. We use a basic adversarial approach with vector space preservation [45] to learn a mapping from an unknown embedding distribution to a known one.

vec2vec is the first method to successfully translate embeddings from the space of one model to another without paired data.1 vec2vec translations achieve cosine similarity as high as 0.92 to the ground-truth vectors in their target embedding spaces and perfect matching on over 8000 shuffled embeddings (without access to the set of possible matches in advance).

To show that our translations preserve not only the relative geometry of embeddings but also the semantics of underlying inputs, we extract information from them using zero-shot attribute inference and inversion, without any knowledge of the model that produced the original embeddings.2
rag_generated_news_article:
Headline:  
Unsupervised Embedding Translation Exposes Widespread Document Leakage Risk Across Medical, Corporate, and Social Data

News Article:  
Over 80% of text embeddings permit partial reconstruction of original documents—including sensitive medical records, Enron corporate emails, and user tweets—according to newly reported empirical evidence. This capacity for partial document recovery is driven by recent technical advancements that enable unsupervised translation of embeddings between distinct model spaces, even in the absence of any paired training data or explicit correspondence. The resulting privacy risk for databases containing only vector representations is both immediate and substantial, as illustrated by direct reconstructions of confidential content from real-world embedding repositories ([source](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/your-embeddings-leaking-hidden-cybersecurity-risk-vector-silveira-jd47f)).

At the core of this development is a newly introduced method, “vec2vec,” presented in the research paper “Translating Embeddings without Pairs” ([arXiv preprint](https://dx.doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.12540)). Vec2vec establishes, for the first time, a technique for translating text embeddings between different, otherwise incompatible vector spaces in a fully unsupervised fashion—requiring no paired data, encoders, or knowledge of matched representations. This approach leverages the conjecture of a universal semantic structure, as articulated in the Strong Platonic Representation Hypothesis: all sufficiently expressive language models, regardless of architecture or training history, implicitly map semantic content into a latent representational structure that can be algorithmically discovered and harnessed.

Traditionally, embeddings generated by models like T5-based GTR and BERT-based GTE are fundamentally incomparable; their vector spaces are defined by divergent architectures, parameters, and training datasets, thus resisting direct interoperability. However, the vec2vec method sidesteps the need for explicit pairings or encoders by learning a shared universal latent representation. Through adversarial losses and cycle consistency, vec2vec encodes samples from arbitrary embedding distributions and enables bidirectional decoding between source and target spaces, preserving vector geometry and semantic information (Figure 1, [Abstract], [Introduction]).

Empirical evaluations confirm the technical viability and precision of this translation. Vec2vec achieves cosine similarity as high as 0.92 when mapping into target embedding spaces, and demonstrates perfect alignment in matching tasks across thousands of embeddings, all without access to original document pairings. Notably, this translation is entirely unsupervised: given only a database of vectors from an unknown model, the method reconstructs representations into a known model’s space (Figure 2, [Introduction]).

This capability has direct real-world ramifications. The primary security concern arises from the possibility that any adversary, with access solely to embedding vectors, can recover latent document characteristics. The practical bridge between technical research and empirical data leakage is now demonstrated: once embeddings are translated into a familiar model space, standard downstream analyses enable content classification, attribute inference, and, crucially, partial document reconstruction ([Abstract],[Anchor Event]). The translation process preserves not only geometric relationships in the vector space, but also sufficient semantic fidelity for zero-shot attribute inference and document inversion—validated on public datasets involving medical data, Enron emails, and user-generated social content.

Fundamentally, the research challenges prevailing assumptions on embedding privacy and model compartmentalization. Prior work had assumed mismatched vector spaces as a barrier to unauthorized data inference. Vec2vec demonstrates that such compartmentalization may be illusory: the universal latent structure permits adversarial translation and cross-model attribute extraction, even when models are architecturally or parametrically distinct and training corpora are unrelated. This undermines the security of current embedding-centric applications in retrieval, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), classification, and clustering, especially where embeddings are treated as non-sensitive, anonymized artifacts.

The paper’s constructive version of the Platonic Representation Hypothesis—demonstrated both formally and empirically—forms the backbone for this universality claim. It opens both opportunities (cross-model interoperability, model-agnostic semantic processing) and significant security risks (unilateral semantic inversion, unauthorized disclosure), necessitating a reassessment of vector database protection and embedding sharing protocols.

Given the technical breakthroughs and the immediate exposure of privacy risks in operational datasets, the expert community is urged to engage directly with the primary source. The preprint, “Translating Embeddings without Pairs”, offers detailed methodology, evaluation, and implications for researchers focused on embedding security, model interoperability, and the foundational principles of semantic representation. Access the full paper [here](https://dx.doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.12540).

title: From brain scans to alloys Teaching AI to make sense of complex research data
abstract: Traditional entropy-based methods—such as cross-entropy loss in classification problems—have long been essential tools for representing the information uncertainty and physical disorder in data and for developing artificial intelligence algorithms. However, the rapid growth of data across various domains has introduced new challenges, particularly the integration of heterogeneous datasets with intrinsic disparities. To address this, we introduce a zentropy-enhanced neural network (ZENN), extending zentropy theory into the data science domain via intrinsic entropy, enabling more effective learning from heterogeneous data sources. ZENN simultaneously learns both energy and intrinsic entropy components, capturing the underlying structure of multisource data. To support this, we redesign the neural network architecture to better reflect the intrinsic properties and variability inherent in diverse datasets. We demonstrate the effectiveness of ZENN on classification tasks and energy landscape reconstructions, showing its superior generalization capabilities and robustness-particularly in predicting high-order derivatives. In image and text classification tasks, ZENN demonstrates superior generalization by introducing a learnable temperature variable that models latent multisource heterogeneity, allowing it to surpass state-of-the-art models on CIFAR-10/100, BBC News, and AG News. As a practical application in materials science, we employ ZENN to reconstruct the Helmholtz energy landscape of Fe3Pt using data generated from density functional theory and capture key material behaviors, including negative thermal expansion and the critical point in the temperature–pressure space. Overall, this work presents a zentropy-grounded framework for data-driven machine learning, positioning ZENN as a versatile and robust approach for scientific problems involving complex, heterogeneous datasets.
introduction: The rise of complex, heterogeneous datasets is transforming data science, enabling new capabilities across fields such as biomedical modeling, materials discovery, climate prediction, and the development of digital twins. These digital replicas of physical systems rely on the seamless integration of real-time and historical data from multiple sources, including sensors, machines, experiments, and simulations (1–3). As a powerful paradigm for empirical discovery, data-driven modeling has been widely applied to solve inverse problems in physics (4), uncover biological mechanisms (5–7), and enable precision diagnostics for complex diseases (8, 9). A key tool in these approaches is entropy, a fundamental concept in thermodynamics and information theory. Entropy provides a framework for modeling uncertainty, disorder, and irreversibility across a wide range of systems, from black holes (10, 11) and ecosystems (12) to digital infrastructures and quantum materials (13–15). According to the second law of thermodynamics, internal processes in closed systems are irreversible, leading to positive entropy production (12, 16). Thermodynamic principles have increasingly influenced deep learning: for instance, neural networks have been developed to construct DFTbased potential energy surfaces (17), Boltzmann Generators embed Boltzmann entropy to predict protein structures (18), and entropy-based frameworks like Thermodynamics-Inspired Explainable Representations and EPR-Net improve interpretability and modeling of high-dimensional non-equilibrium systems (19–21). Entropy-driven objectives—such as cross-entropy loss—have been widely applied in machine learning(22–25), particularly for classification tasks (26). However, traditional entropy-based methods typically provide only macroscopic or microscopic approximations and often fail to account for internal disparities when fusing multisource data. These limitations are particularly pronounced for real-world datasets, which vary in scale, resolution, measurement conditions, and statistical properties—posing challenges for conventional machine learning methods that assume data homogeneity and stationarity (27). To overcome these challenges, zentropy theory introduces a thermodynamically consistent framework that incorporates intrinsic entropy, capturing variability across datasets due to environmental, experimental, or contextual differences (28, 29). Originally developed to predict magnetic phase diagrams in fcc-Ce (30) and L12-Fe3Pt (31), zentropy has since been applied to a wide range of materials, including strongly correlated systems (32–34), liquid and melting phases (35), plasticity (36), and superconductivity (37). Despite its success in physics, zentropy’s reliance on DFT for computing configuration-level entropy has limited its application in broader data-driven domains. Nevertheless, as a foundational framework for integrating heterogeneous data, zentropy remains essential for ensuring thermodynamic consistency and enabling more accurate, comprehensive models. Building on this foundation, we introduce a zentropyenhanced neural network (ZENN) that embeds zentropy theory into deep learning to enable entropy-aware learning from complex, heterogeneous datasets. Unlike Shannon entropy, zentropy’s thermodynamic basis captures domainspecific complexity, providing richer structure for modeling multi-source data. By incorporating zentropy into neural network design, ZENN learns both energy and intrinsic entropy components, capturing the underlying structure of heterogeneous datasets. This approach extends the applicability of zentropy beyond quantum and statistical mechanics, offering a robust framework for integrating heterogeneous, real-world data and improving predictive performance in classification, energy landscape reconstruction, and other scientific applications. While zentropy has been widely used in forward modeling, where it plays a critical role in predicting system behavior, its integration into data-driven approaches for inverse problems remains unexplored. This paper addresses this gap by embedding zentropy theory into data science, advancing the application of entropy-based modeling within the context of data-driven techniques. More specifically, to broaden the applicability of zentropy theory beyond its traditional domain, we developed ZENN—a framework designed to integrate multi-source data, reconstruct energy landscapes, and infer system properties. We benchmarked ZENN against cross-entropy loss using state-of-the-art models for both image and text classification tasks and energy landscape reconstruction. In all cases, ZENN consistently outperformed cross-entropy loss in terms of accuracy and stability. To further validate its capabilities, ZENN was applied to the Fe3Pt compound, successfully capturing its negative thermal expansion (NTE) behavior and accurately predicting its critical point in the temperature-pressure/volume spaces. These results demonstrate ZENN’s strong generalization ability and its enhanced performance in estimating highorder derivatives—an essential feature for modeling complex thermodynamic systems

Headline:  
Zentropy-Embedded Neural Networks (ZENN) Advance Entropy-Aware AI for Multisource Data Integration

News Article:  
Zentropy-Embedded Neural Networks (ZENN) mark a pivotal advancement in AI’s ability to discern signal from uncertainty, fundamentally by integrating thermodynamic concepts—specifically, intrinsic entropy—into the neural network’s architecture. This innovation equips AI systems to better capture the underlying structure of multisource data, as highlighted in recent professional reports and underscored by LinkedIn coverage: “ZENN (Zentropy-Embedded Neural Networks) helps AI learn the difference between signal and uncertainty by building thermodynamic ideas into its operation.” Central to ZENN’s approach is the explicit modeling of intrinsic entropy, allowing for robust and entropy-aware learning strategies that navigate the challenges posed by complex and heterogeneous datasets.

Historically, entropy-based methods—especially cross-entropy loss—have been cornerstone tools in machine learning, providing a theoretical linkage between information uncertainty, physical disorder, and learning efficiency. Yet, these traditional approaches are generally optimized for homogeneous data environments and offer only coarse macroscopic or microscopic approximations. In the rapidly evolving landscape of data science, however, practitioners are increasingly confronted with datasets characterized by intrinsic disparities in scale, measurement conditions, and statistical properties. These disparities undermine the reliability and generalizability of models grounded solely in classic entropy constructs.   

ZENN’s architecture extends beyond conventional entropy by embedding zentropy theory, a thermodynamically consistent framework originally formulated to capture intrinsic entropy in physical systems. Unlike Shannon entropy, which abstracts uncertainty in broad terms, zentropy is grounded in the explicit thermodynamic variability present in multisource data, reflecting environmental, experimental, or contextual differences encountered in empirical scientific domains. Through this construct, ZENN captures both the energy and intrinsic entropy components of input data, ensuring a refined capacity for distinguishing signal from various forms of noise and structural heterogeneity. As described in the primary research, leveraging intrinsic entropy enables ZENN to model multisource heterogeneity, directly supporting its superior generalization capabilities.

The architectural enhancements of ZENN include the integration of a learnable temperature variable—anchoring the model’s responsiveness to latent heterogeneity among multiple data sources. This design feature facilitates the simultaneous learning of energy and intrinsic entropy, providing a mechanism to faithfully capture the underlying statistical structure of complex datasets. The outcomes are particularly evident in empirical benchmarks, where ZENN consistently surpasses state-of-the-art models employing traditional cross-entropy loss across both image (CIFAR-10/100) and text (BBC News, AG News) classification tasks. The explicit modeling of intrinsic entropy plays a crucial role, as it systematically addresses the limitations of traditional approaches when applied to heterogeneous datasets.

Beyond classification, ZENN demonstrates significance in reconstructing high-dimensional energy landscapes—exemplified in its application to materials science. The model has been employed to reconstruct the Helmholtz energy landscape of Fe3Pt using data generated from density functional theory (DFT). In this context, ZENN successfully captures complex material behaviors such as negative thermal expansion and accurately infers critical points within temperature–pressure spaces. These capabilities highlight the utility of embedding intrinsic entropy for scientific inference tasks that require robust estimation of high-order derivatives in settings marked by data diversity and structural complexity.

Zentropy theory’s grounding in thermodynamics, and its prior application to quantum and statistical mechanics, positions ZENN as an adaptable and scientifically rigorous solution for integrating heterogeneous data. By directly embedding zentropy, the model extends the theory’s reach from physical systems modeling into the broader domain of data-driven approaches, particularly for inverse problems where heterogeneity and uncertainty are inherent.

With the proliferation of multisource, real-world data across domains such as biomedical modeling, materials discovery, and climate prediction, the need for AI frameworks capable of entropy-aware learning is acute. ZENN sets a precedent by explicitly connecting the core thermodynamic concept of intrinsic entropy to a neural network’s operational efficacy in distinguishing signal from uncertainty—an advance that is immediately salient to the research community contending with data heterogeneity.

For those seeking to understand the technical underpinnings and implications of zentropy-grounded machine learning, direct engagement with the full research paper is strongly encouraged. The published work delineates the methods, benchmarking results, and domain-specific applications, offering valuable insights for computational scientists seeking to extend entropy-aware frameworks to new classes of heterogeneous data-driven discovery.

Access the full research publication at https://dx.doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2511227122.

title: Discrete spatial diffusion models data while obeying scientific principles
abstract: Generative diffusion models have achieved remarkable success in producing high- quality images. However, these models typically operate in continuous intensity spaces, diffusing independently across pixels and color channels. As a result, they are fundamentally ill-suited for applications involving inherently discrete quantities—such as particle counts or material units—that are constrained by strict conservation laws like mass conservation, limiting their applicability in scientific workflows. To address this limitation, we propose Discrete Spatial Diffusion (DSD), a framework based on a continuous-time, discrete-state jump stochastic process that operates directly in discrete spatial domains while strictly preserving particle counts in both forward and reverse diffusion processes. By using spatial diffusion to achieve particle conservation, we introduce stochasticity naturally through a dis- crete formulation. We demonstrate the expressive flexibility of DSD by performing image synthesis, class conditioning, and image inpainting across standard image benchmarks, while exactly conditioning total image intensity. We validate DSD on two challenging scientific applications: porous rock microstructures and lithium- ion battery electrodes, demonstrating its ability to generate structurally realistic samples under strict mass conservation constraints, with quantitative evaluation using state-of-the-art metrics for transport and electrochemical performance.
introduction:
Introduction

Diffusion-based generative models have emerged as powerful tools for high-quality image generation [36, 66, 69]. Typically, these models inject noise into the images, then learn to reverse this noise- adding process to recover meaningful structure. In most frameworks, this is based on an Itô Stochastic Differential Equation (SDE) with Gaussian noise. While effective for many vision tasks, these approaches inherently assume continuous pixel intensities, which can cause difficulty when dealing with the discrete nature of many datasets. Tasks beyond vision, such as those in the physical sciences have many applications which require discrete physical quantities, such as particle counts in a simulation, or phases in materials microstructure. Conservation of total quantities is critical for scientific applications, and so generative modeling which can operate under constrained, discrete pixel intensities would enable scientifically grounded and physically consistent synthesis. Such a capability might also prove useful within vision tasks, such as inpainting and super-resolution.

Scientific and engineering studies of the natural world using computational techniques often involve discrete variables in space and/or time. On microscopic scales, everyday materials exhibit extremely complex structural patterns which encode the history of their formation, and play a large role in how the material functions on a macroscopic level. An important and wide-reaching field of study is materials microstructure, which is used in materials design [35], forensic analysis, hydrology [7], energy storage [65], and even medicine, such as in studies of bone structure [53]. For example,

∗Los Alamos National Laboratory

crystal grain shapes can give rise to complex stress patterns which affect the yield strength of a metal [10]. Materials microstructures are frequently represented by a small number of discrete phases that describe their underlying chemical structures. In sandstone, for example, the overall arrangement of nanocrystals is highly disordered, and gives rise to complex pore structures, through which subsurface water flows. This microstructure has an enormous influence on the rate of transport of fluids and contaminants [6]. Microstructure of electrodes is also known to have an immense impact on the characteristics of electrochemical devices [58]. Small changes in thermodynamic properties can cause drastic changes in microstructure, such as in stainless steels [79], requiring the study of microstructure as a function of phase contents. Furthermore, gathering real-world data is often complex and expensive; decades of work have been applied to computational modeling of the generation and consequences of microstructure prior to the widespread popularization of machine learning [72]. While diffusion models have been constructed for microstructure [4, 25, 37, 46, 47, 52, 80], they have not handled exact porosity (intensity) constraints.

In this work, we introduce Discrete Spatial Diffusion (DSD), a discrete-state Markov chain-based diffusion framework in which the forward process redistributes discrete units of intensity in space (Fig. 1c). Unlike previous diffusion models, DSD exactly preserves total intensity throughout both the forward and reverse phases, ensuring that global properties—such as mass fractions—are exactly conserved by representing them in terms of conserved particles. We demonstrate that DSD enables scientific applications and can extend the capabilities of conventional image processing tasks, like image generation and inpainting in discrete domains. By directly modeling discrete transitions, DSD enables generative modeling under conservation laws, allowing models that specialize for constrained conditions in scientific applications and beyond.
rag_generated_news_article:
Headline:  
Discrete Spatial Diffusion: A Generative Model Grounded in Mass-Conserving Reaction–Diffusion Theory

News Article:  
A rigorous theoretical framework for mass-conserving reaction–diffusion systems has long been central to the study of pattern formation and material behavior across the physical sciences, providing the backbone for research in fields such as materials science, hydrology, and electrochemical engineering [Real-World Anchor Event, Result 4]. Conservation laws, particularly mass conservation, are not mere formalities but essential constraints that govern the evolution and steady-state properties of such systems. This requirement for strict adherence to physical conservation principles poses profound challenges for computational modeling and generative methods tasked with synthesizing physically plausible microstructures and simulating natural phenomena.

Bridging this theoretical foundation, recent work introduces Discrete Spatial Diffusion (DSD) [Abstract; Introduction; Citation], a generative modeling framework designed explicitly to respect discrete and conserved quantities—a direct response to the requirements laid out by mass-conserving reaction–diffusion systems. Traditional diffusion-based generative models, despite their performance in continuous intensity domains (as in image synthesis), are fundamentally unsuited for domains in which the discrete nature of the data and global conservation laws are inviolable. These classical models, typically formulated over continuous state spaces and governed by Itô stochastic differential equations, inject Gaussian noise across pixels or color channels independently, failing both to model discrete quantities and to enforce global invariants such as mass conservation.

DSD replaces the continuous-state, additive-noise paradigm with a continuous-time, discrete-state jump stochastic process. In this formalism, the forward process redistributes discrete units of intensity—or, by analogy, discrete particles—across the spatial domain. Crucially, DSD is constructed such that, throughout both the forward (diffusion) and reverse (generation) processes, the total number of particles is strictly conserved [Abstract]. In Markov chain terms, DSD generates a trajectory in which every configuration is a redistribution—not a creation or annihilation—of discrete, conserved units. This aligns precisely with the physical constraints highlighted by the mass-conserving reaction–diffusion theoretical framework: transitions occur without any change in the global number of entities (masses, particles, species, etc.).

This discrete-state formulation is not merely a theoretical exercise but is validated across both standard image benchmarks and domain-specific scientific workflows. Empirically, DSD supports image synthesis, class conditioning, and inpainting while exactly conditioning on total image intensity—a proxy for discrete quantity conservation [Abstract]. These capabilities are demonstrated on standard imaging tasks as well as on scientifically significant applications, including the generation of porous rock microstructures and lithium-ion battery electrodes, where physical realism depends intrinsically on the conservation of mass and structure.

The implications for scientific modeling are direct. In domains such as materials microstructure analysis—where the arrangement of discrete material phases impacts macroscopic properties like yield strength or fluid transport—the inability to enforce conservation laws renders many generative approaches unsuitable [Introduction]. By design, DSD inhabits a generative modeling regime that is not only physically meaningful but also technically necessary for simulating systems subject to strict conservation of species or quantities, a trait previously unaddressed in diffusion-based microstructure modeling.

Formally, DSD’s integration of a discrete jump process ensures that global constraints—such as mass fractions or particle counts—are preserved, in full alignment with the physical principles of mass-conserving reaction–diffusion systems. This represents a technically sound alignment between generative modeling methodology and the foundational demands of physical systems biology, materials science, and engineering, as articulated by contemporary theoretical research. DSD’s adoption explicitly acknowledges that generative models required for scientific applications must move beyond the continuous, Gaussian-noise regime toward a framework that honors the discrete, conserved, and stochastic nature of material microstructure and evolution.

The DSD model thus opens the door for physically consistent and quantitatively rigorous generative modeling in a broad class of applications where conservation laws are non-negotiable. For computational physicists and materials scientists seeking to synthesize, complete, or augment structures while guaranteeing adherence to fundamental conservation principles, DSD provides a mechanism that integrates the latest developments in generative modeling directly with the established theory of mass-conserving reaction–diffusion systems.

For full technical details and evaluation results—including quantitative benchmarks for transport and electrochemical performance—readers are encouraged to access the original research at [https://dx.doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.01917](https://dx.doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2505.01917). This work establishes a new baseline for generative methods that are not only empirically powerful but also grounded in the rigorous, conservation-centric theory at the heart of scientific modeling.

title: Decoding black box AI with humanreadable data descriptions and influence
abstract:
Jaeyeon Bae* Ulsan National Institute of Science and Technology(UNIST) {chaerikim, qowodussla, taehwankim}@unist.ac.kr

Deep learning models have been successful in many areas but understanding their behaviors still remains a black-box. Most prior explain- able AI (XAI) approaches have focused on in- terpreting and explaining how models make predictions. In contrast, we would like to un- derstand how data can be explained with deep learning model training and propose a novel approach to understand the data via one of the most common media - language - so that hu- mans can easily understand. Our approach pro- poses a pipeline to generate textual descrip- tions that can explain the data with large lan- guage models by incorporating external knowl- edge bases. However, generated data descrip- tions may still include irrelevant information, so we introduce to exploit influence estimation to choose the most informative textual descrip- tions, along with the CLIP score. Furthermore, based on the phenomenon of cross-modal trans- ferability, we propose a novel benchmark task named cross-modal transfer classification to examine the effectiveness of our textual descrip- tions. In the experiment of zero-shot setting, we show that our textual descriptions are more effective than other baseline descriptions, and furthermore, we successfully boost the perfor- mance of the model trained only on images across all nine image classification datasets. These results are further supported by evalu- ation using GPT-4o. Through our approach, we may gain insights into the inherent inter- pretability of the decision-making process of the model.
introduction:
Introduction

Deep learning models have successfully been ap- plied to various fields and achieved high perfor- mance (Huang et al., 2017; Vaswani et al., 2017; He et al., 2016; Dosovitskiy et al., 2020). Despite the rapid performance improvement, understand- ing their behaviors remains a black-box. While most prior explainable AI (XAI) approaches have focused on interpreting and explaining how models make predictions, there are few attempts to explain the data. We would like to understand data since it is one of the most important elements of deep learning performance. Among deep learning models, we try to describe image classes with humaninterpretable language. For example, in the case of frog class: smooth, moist skin with coloration ranging from green to brown, often featuring various patterns and markings. Recent studies (Menon and Vondrick, 2023; Maniparambil et al., 2023; Pratt et al., 2023) have utilized large language models (LLMs), pre-trained on vast datasets, to generate textual descriptions that aid vision model performance. However, identifying descriptions that are truly informative and semantically aligned with the class remains a key challenge. To solve this problem, we propose novel approach to identify the most helpful textsfor model training among those generated by the LLM, using influence estimation and CLIP scores. Influence estimation (Koh and Liang, 2017; Pruthi et al., 2020) calculates the positive or negative impact of each training image in predicting a test sample, while CLIP scores measure the similarity between an image and text. Unlike most previous research that applies influence scores only to images, we extend this method to texts by integrating CLIP scores to identify helpful texts for model training. We name the helpful text as proponent text and the score for determining the proponent text as Influence scores For Texts (IFT).

To obtain the proponent texts, we first write down all the features of each image class with GPT- 3.5 (Brown et al., 2020). Instead of merely query- ing the LLM for class features, we employ a two- stage prompting process using Wikipedia urls to integrate external knowledge bases. This approach not only compensates for potential gaps in detailed knowledge of LLM but also helps mitigate halluci-

These authors contributed equally to this work.

Figure 1: Overview of our framework. (1) ’Generating Textual Descriptions of Each Dataset’ process generates the textual descriptions that can explain each class well from given Wikipedia urls, pre-defined prompts, and class names (2) ’Choosing Proponent Texts using IFT’ process determines the proponent texts using IFT. (3) ’Cross-Modal Transfer Classification’ process first trains model with images, then further trains it with proponent texts.

nations. Among the generated textual descriptions, IFT filters out unhelpful information and retains only proponent texts. Furthermore, leveraging the concept of cross-modal transferability (Zhang et al., 2023), which allows text to serve as input for mod- els originally trained with images, we apply this principle to our approach. We retrain the image- trained model using proponent texts, assigning weights based on the IFT. We call this ’cross-modal transfer classification’. Compared to image-only training and baselines that generate textual descrip- tions, our approach achieves superior performance on most of the nine image datasets, demonstrating that our textual descriptions are more helpful in model training than other baselines. Additionally, we evaluate the helpfulness, informativeness, and relevance of the generated descriptions using GPT- 4o (Hurst et al., 2024) and find that our method consistently outperforms all baselines across these criteria. Figure 1 shows the overview of our frame- work.

In sum, our contributions are as follows:

We propose a novel approach to identify help- ful textual descriptions that effectively explain each image class, achieving superior perfor- mance in the zero-shot setting compared to baseline descriptions.

By using the proposed IFT defined as the sum of the influence score and the CLIP score, we can pinpoint the most helpful textual descrip- tions for model training. Proponent texts se- lected through IFT contain only relevant in- formation that aids in accurate image classifi- cation. Furthermore, we may get an inherent interpretability of where the black-box model focuses on during training.

We propose cross-modal transfer classifica- tion as a novel benchmark task based on the cross-modal transferability phenomenon. Ex- tensive experiments with nine datasets show

that the proponent texts are informative and describe each image class well.
rag_generated_news_article:
Headline:  
Variability and Informativeness in LLM-Generated Descriptions Significantly Enhance Cross-Modal Image Classification

News Article:  
Recent findings underscore that variability in ChatGPT-generated articles positively impacts model accuracy, establishing a direct link between textual diversity and improved performance in downstream classification tasks. This key outcome, observed in technical studies on generative AI-aided data augmentation, provides a compelling anchor for ongoing advances in explainable AI (XAI), especially efforts focused on harnessing large language models (LLMs) to benefit vision models through textual descriptions [Real-World Anchor Event; https://www.osti.gov/servlets/purl/1997756].

In this context, Jaeyeon Bae and colleagues at UNIST introduce a novel technical framework aimed at optimizing the selection of LLM-generated textual descriptions for image classes. Rather than merely leveraging arbitrary language outputs, the methodology pioneers influence estimation and CLIP score integration to systematically identify the most informative, semantically aligned textual explanations for model training [Abstract; Introduction]. This approach not only resonates with the established finding that greater variability and longer synthesized texts aid model accuracy, but also elevates the informativity and relevance of generated descriptions in cross-modal settings.

The technical significance lies in moving the XAI conversation beyond interpreting model predictions, directing attention towards how training data—specifically, textual representations sourced from LLMs—can be explained, filtered, and augmented for model improvement. Here, the research operationalizes a multi-stage pipeline: first, LLMs (specifically GPT-3.5) generate initial feature descriptions for each image class by integrating structured information from external knowledge bases such as Wikipedia. This two-stage prompting mechanism compensates for potential knowledge gaps and mitigates LLM hallucinations by anchoring the generative process in curated reference material [Introduction].

However, not all LLM-generated texts hold equal utility. Prior studies, including the referenced anchor event, highlight that synthesized data, when varied and lengthened, can enhance classification accuracy. Bae et al. extend this logic by developing a discriminative mechanism: Influence scores For Texts (IFT), defined as the sum of two components—conventional influence estimation (measuring each description’s impact on a model’s prediction, extrapolated from techniques such as those by Koh and Liang) and CLIP scores (quantifying semantic alignment between text and corresponding images). The rigorous application of IFT enables the filtration of unhelpful or irrelevant descriptions, preserving only those that are demonstrably conducive to accurate image classification [Abstract; Introduction].

This informs a critical bridge: while generic generative augmentation introduces beneficial variability, as per the anchor study, Bae et al. ensure that only those textual variants exerting positive, measurable influence—verified by influence estimation and CLIP scoring—are retained. The resulting proponent texts optimally balance variability, informativeness, and semantic alignment, extending the observed benefits of LLM variability with added discrimination and technical rigor.

To quantitatively validate this pipeline, the researchers introduce “cross-modal transfer classification,” a benchmark predicated on cross-modal transferability. Here, a model is initially trained on images and subsequently retrained on proponent texts—those textual descriptions selected via IFT. Empirical results spanning nine image classification datasets demonstrate a consistent performance boost over baseline methods, including traditional image-only training and LLM-generated baselines without influence-based filtering [Abstract; Introduction]. Notably, the superiority of these proponent texts manifests not only in traditional zero-shot settings, but is corroborated by GPT-4o evaluation on dimensions of helpfulness, informativeness, and relevance.

This research substantiates the hypothesis, anchored in real-world AI-driven data generation studies, that greater variability and length in synthesized texts confer technical advantages, but further contributes a principled framework for maximizing this benefit via targeted selection metrics. It formalizes and quantifies the impact of LLM-synthesized diversity—not simply as a byproduct of generative abundance but as a tunable asset in vision model training pipelines.

The implications for the computer vision and XAI communities are multifold. First, there is now a pathway for more interpretable, data-centric augmentation of deep learning models, complementing extant model-centric interpretability approaches. Second, the IFT and cross-modal transfer classification paradigm invite further investigation into cross-modal explanation, both as a research direction and a practical tool for robust model development. Finally, the demonstrated link between principled text selection strategies and quantifiable accuracy gains provides both a technical blueprint and an actionable methodology for practitioners seeking to bridge LLM and vision domains.

For expert readers wishing to examine the methodologies, benchmarks, and source code underlying this work, access to the full research paper is recommended: https://dx.doi.org/10.18653/v1/2025.emnlp-main.1717. This study represents a tangible advance in the technical interface between explainable AI, generative augmentation, and cross-modal learning.

title: New framework helps AI systems recover from mistakes and find optimal solutions
abstract: We introduce a new approach to agent programming, the development of LLM- based agents. Current approaches to agent programming often entangle two aspects of agent design: the core workflow logic and the inference-time strategy (e.g., tree search). We introduce probabilistic angelic nondeterminism (PAN), a programming model that disentangles these two concerns, allowing the programmer to describe the agent workflow and independently experiment with different inference-time strategies by simply changing a few inputs. We provide an implementation of PAN in Python as the ENCOMPASS framework, which uses a Python decorator to compile agent workflow programs into a search space. We present three case studies that demonstrate how the framework lets the programmer quickly improve the reliability of an agent and easily switch between different inference-time strategies, all with little additional coding.
introduction:
Introduction

Recent work has shown the power of scaling inference-time compute for LLMs [1, 2], where popular strategies include best-of-N sampling [3, 4, 5], refinement [6, 7], and tree search [8, 9]. In LLM-based agents — systems that define how LLMs and other components interact to solve a task — these same strategies have become common ways of improving performance and reliability. Furthermore, several works have demonstrated the utility of applying sophisticated search and backtracking strategies in AI agents to improve performance in various tasks [8, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14].

While various frameworks have been developed to simplify the low-level interaction between the program and the LLM [15, 16, 17, 18], a framework for agent inference-time strategies has been absent. Our goal is to develop an inference-time strategy framework: a framework that makes it easy to experiment with different inference-time strategies independently of the design and implementation of the underlying agent workflow. Such a framework is intended not to replace, but to be used in conjunction with LLM prompting and tool use frameworks, such as LangChain [15] or DSPy [16].

We target “program-in-control” style agents, where one defines the workflow in code and uses the LLM to accomplish specific subtasks [14, 19, 20, 21, 22].2 In these agents, inference-time strategies have traditionally been limited to sampling and refinement loops [6, 7, 19, 20], whereas more sophisticated strategies such as beam search and tree search have been rarely explored [14].

∗Work performed as a consultant for Asari AI 2This “program-in-control” style contrasts with the “LLM-in-control” style where the LLM decides the full

sequence of operations (tool calls) in the workflow [8, 9, 10, 11, 12, 13].

Figure 1: An ENCOMPASS program specifies an agent workflow, which is compiled into a search space object, and inference-time scaling is accomplished through search over the nondeterministic execution paths of the agent workflow.

We identify the key bottleneck to be the entanglement of the inference-time scaling strategy with the core workflow logic when programming the agent. Programmers typically bake the inference- time strategy into the agent workflow [14, 19, 20, 22], which is inflexible, reduces readability, and limits the kinds of inference-time strategies that can be easily implemented. Therefore, we aim to design a framework that cleanly separates the representation of the core workflow logic from the inference-time scaling strategy. The programmer could then make minimal modifications to their agent to flexibly experiment with different inference-time strategies. Also, different agents would no longer require custom implementations of the same inference-time strategy, but can instead reuse a common implementation.

Our key insight is that inference-time strategies can be viewed as instances of search over different execution paths of a nondeterministic program. We developed the ENCOMPASS Python programming framework (“enhancing agents with compiled agent search”), depicted in Figure 1. Figure 1a and Figure 1b show an agent program and its corresponding workflow, respectively. The user specifies the “locations of unreliability” in their agent source code using branchpoint() statements. A location of unreliability is an operation such as an LLM call where repeated invocations produce outputs of varying quality. Since these different outputs give rise to multiple possible futures of the program’s execution, the program has a tree of possible execution paths. ENCOMPASS compiles the program into a search space object (Figure 1c) so that search can be conducted over this tree of execution paths to find the path with the highest score (Figure 1d). We call this programming model probabilistic angelic nondeterminism (PAN). As a form of angelic nondeterminism [23], PAN lets the programmer write their program pretending the unreliable operations always produce good outputs, and the runtime searches the space of possible execution paths for one where the operations indeed produced good outputs.

Our work makes the following concrete contributions:

We introduce the PAN programming model (Section 2.1), which uses angelic nondetermin- ism to separate inference-time algorithms (search policy) from the underlying logic of the agent (specification of the search space).

We present ENCOMPASS, a Python library that implements PAN (Section 2.2), providing 1. primitives like branchpoint() that the programmer can use inside their ENCOMPASS function, 2. a Python function decorator that compiles an ENCOMPASS function into a search space object at run-time, and 3. common search algorithms, as well as an interface for implementing custom search algorithms.

We illustrate how ENCOMPASS provides a unifying framework for common inference-time strategies and agentic patterns, which are special cases of search over nondeterministic execution paths of ENCOMPASS programs (Section 3). ENCOMPASS also provides a natural generalization of these inference-time strategies.

We present case studies showing how ENCOMPASS enables easy experimentation of various inference-time search strategies over an underlying agent workflow, allowing one to quickly identify the best-performing strategy (Section 4). ENCOMPASS opens up new possibilities for inference-time scaling of program-in-control style agents, where inference-time strategies that were previously considered too cumbersome to implement are now made possible by ENCOMPASS.

Headline:  
ENCOMPASS: Python Library Revolutionizes Agentic AI by Decoupling Workflow and Inference-Time Strategy Ahead of NeurIPS 2025

News Article:  
ENCOMPASS, a recently introduced Python library, offers a transformative approach to agent programming by enabling rapid experimentation with advanced search methods for large language model (LLM)-based agents. Marking a pivotal development in agentic AI research, ENCOMPASS will be presented at NeurIPS 2025, a top-tier conference recognized for spotlighting critical progress in artificial intelligence. This presentation underscores ENCOMPASS’s role in advancing AI systems that can systematically plan, explore, and discover new approaches—core themes directly aligned with NeurIPS’s emphasis on scalable, reproducible, and flexible AI architectures ([Real-World Anchor Event](https://asari.ai/blog/encompass)).

ENCOMPASS emerges in response to persistent technical limitations in the domain of agent programming. Prevailing approaches tend to interweave two distinct concerns: the core agent workflow logic—defining how the agent should behave—and the inference-time scaling strategies used to improve reliability and performance, such as tree search or best-of-N sampling ([Abstract]; [Introduction]). This entanglement reduces both modularity and reproducibility and constrains innovation, as modifying an agent’s inference-time strategy typically requires substantial rewrites to core logic.

The ENCOMPASS framework directly addresses this bottleneck by operationalizing a new programming model—probabilistic angelic nondeterminism (PAN). In this paradigm, the agent’s workflow and the search policy applied during inference-time are represented as explicitly separate entities. Programmers annotate locations of unreliability in the agent code—such as LLM calls that can yield variable-quality outputs—using a branchpoint() primitive. These points yield a combinatorially large tree of possible execution paths, corresponding to the diverse outputs of nondeterministic subroutines ([Abstract]).

At runtime, ENCOMPASS compiles the annotated agent program into a search space object. This object abstracts all nondeterministic execution paths, enabling search algorithms to efficiently explore and select the path that optimizes a predefined scoring function ([Introduction]). The “angelic” aspect of PAN refers to the programmer’s ability to code under the assumption that unreliable operations will return desirable results; the search process then finds such outcomes within the actual variability of execution ([Abstract]). ENCOMPASS’s Python implementation provides: (1) branchpoint() primitives for marking nondeterministic operations, (2) a function decorator that compiles these into a search space object, and (3) a suite of built-in and extensible search algorithms, ranging from best-of-N sampling to sophisticated tree and beam search methods ([Abstract]; [Introduction]).

This architectural decoupling yields significant immediate benefits for research and engineering in agentic AI:

- **Modularity and Experimentation**: Researchers and practitioners can alter inference-time strategies—such as swapping sampling for tree search—by changing a few input parameters, without modifying the core agent logic ([Abstract], Section 4 referenced in sources). This enables systematic benchmarking of agentic workflows across multiple strategy variants and facilitates the discovery of optimal methods for new tasks.
- **Reproducibility and Flexibility**: By adopting a unified abstraction for search over execution paths, commonly used inference-time strategies are formalized as special cases within ENCOMPASS. Different agents can reuse generic implementations of inference strategies, minimizing redundant or bespoke coding efforts ([Introduction]).
- **Scalability of Agentic Patterns**: ENCOMPASS generalizes the implementation of complex agentic patterns—such as decomposition, refinement, and backtracking—across diverse LLM-based agents, opening up avenues for inference-time scaling that were previously intractable to realize due to architectural coupling ([Abstract]; [Introduction]).

Case studies cited in the ENCOMPASS research highlight the immediate utility of the framework: programmers are able to rapidly iterate between inference-time search strategies, empirically identify superior agent behaviors, and enhance agent reliability—all with minimal changes to high-level workflow specifications ([Abstract], Section 4). These results affirm ENCOMPASS’s potential to accelerate algorithmic innovation and standardize experimentation, two recurring themes at NeurIPS.

The presentation of ENCOMPASS at NeurIPS 2025 situates the framework at the center of ongoing efforts to build AI agents with advanced planning and exploration capabilities—a primary concern for the machine learning research community. ENCOMPASS’s explicit separation of agent workflow and inference-time decision-making directly supports the development of reproducible, modular, and adaptive AI, and establishes a technical foundation for future work in composable and robust agentic architectures ([Real-World Anchor Event]).

For researchers and technologists seeking a principled, scalable avenue to benchmark and extend inference strategies in LLM-based agents, access to the ENCOMPASS research is strongly encouraged. The full technical details and case studies present a foundation upon which the next generation of flexible and reliable agentic AI systems can be built. Read the [ENCOMPASS research paper](https://openreview.net/pdf?id=IKVkpjSJzJ) to examine the framework’s core contributions and their implications for the future of AI agent programming.

title: An AI approach for singleimagebased 3D character animation with preserved proportions
abstract: Reconstructing object deformation from a single image remains a significant challenge in computer vision and graphics. Existing methods typically rely on multi-view video to recover deformation, limiting their applicability under constrained scenarios. To address this, we propose DeformSplat, a novel framework that effectively guides 3D Gaussian deformation from only a single image. Our method introduces two main technical contributions. First, we present Gaussian-to-Pixel Matching which bridges the domain gap between 3D Gaussian representations and 2D pixel observations. This enables robust deformation guidance from sparse visual cues. Second, we propose Rigid Part Segmentation consisting of initialization and refinement. This segmentation explicitly identifies rigid regions, crucial for maintaining geometric coherence during deformation. By combining these two techniques, our approach can reconstruct consistent deformations from a single image. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms existing methods and naturally extends to various applications, such as frame interpolation and interactive object manipulation. Project page : https://vision3d-lab.github.io/deformsplat
introduction:
Introduction

Reconstructing object deformation from visual data is essential for creating realistic and immersive content in various media fields, such as virtual reality (VR), film, and gaming [Shuai et al. 2022]. As part of this effort, recent works have explored photorealistic rendering of deformable objects, aiming to better capture their ap- pearance and motion over time [Lu et al. 2024a; Wu et al. 2024b]. Although these methods have demonstrated strong capabilities for dynamic scene reconstruction, they often rely on multi-view or tem- porally continuous video data. Such data can be difficult to capture in real-world settings, which limits their practical applicability. This motivates the development of methods that reconstruct deformation from minimal visual inputs.

Toachievehigh-qualityandefficientreconstructionofdeformable objects, recent research has focused on scene representations that support both photorealism and editability. Among these, 3D Gauss- ian Splatting (3DGS) [Kerbl et al. 2023] has gained attention for its high-quality rendering and fast inference. Unlike implicit neural representations, such as Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) [Mildenhall

SIGGRAPH Asia 2025, December 15–18, 2025, Hong Kong, Hong Kong.

Trovato et al.

et al. 2021], 3DGS explicitly models geometry, making it both in- terpretable and easy to manipulate. Motivated by these advantages, several subsequent works have explored Gaussian-based scene edit- ing. For example, several approaches leveraging diffusion models enable intuitive editing guided by text prompts [Wu et al. 2024a] or reference images [Mei et al. 2024]. While these approaches enable intuitive edits, diffusion models often produce inconsistent results, as ambiguous text prompts lead to varied interpretations and limited geometric control. Another recent method, GESI [Luo et al. 2024], aims to address detailed geometric editing directly using a single reference image. However, it encounters difficulties in preserving intricate geometry under long-range deformations, often altering the original structure significantly. These limitations motivate our key research question: Can 3D Gaussians be deformed from a single image while preserving the original geometry?

In this work, we aim to deform a pre-reconstructed 3D Gaussian representation using only a single target image depicting a defor- mation, as illustrated in Fig. 1. Our setting is challenging, as we aim to deform 3D Gaussians using only a single RGB image, unlike conventional methods that rely on richer inputs such as multiple views or video sequences. The absence of depth and camera pose information further complicates the deformation process in our case. This constrained setting gives rise to two key challenges. The first challenge is determining how and in which direction the Gaussians should deform when only a single viewpoint is available. This is difficult because a single image provides only partial observations of the 3D structure, making it hard to infer meaningful deformation cues. To extract meaningful deformation cues under this constraint, it is essential to establish reliable correspondences between the 2D image and the 3D Gaussians. The second challenge is to prevent overfitting to the single input image, which can result in unwanted geometric distortions due to the lack of depth or multi-view con- straints. Thus, preserving original geometry is crucial, especially in rigid regions that should remain unchanged during deformation.

To address these challenges, we propose a novel framework called DeformSplat, consisting of two main components: Gaussian-to- Pixel Matching and Rigid Part Segmentation. Gaussian-to-Pixel Matching aims to guide the deformation by linking visually similar regions between the 3D Gaussians and the target image. Specifically, we render multi-view images from the 3D Gaussians and compute pixel-wise correspondences between each rendered image and the target image using an image matcher. Based on the pixel-to-pixel matching, we select the viewpoint with the largest visual overlap, and translate its correspondences into Gaussian-to-Pixel mappings. This step provides an essential basis for deformation, enabling the Gaussians to reflect the geometry depicted in the target image.

To further ensure geometric consistency, we propose Rigid Part Segmentation that explicitly identifies rigid regions within the Gaussian representation. To achieve this, our method first initial- izes rigid groups based on Gaussian-to-Pixel correspondences and then iteratively refines these groups during optimization. The seg- mentation is used in a rigidity-aware optimization that regularizes rigid and non-rigid regions differently to preserve geometry. Conse- quently, we achieve superior performance than previous SOTA and generalize to applications such as frame interpolation and interac- tive object manipulation.

SIGGRAPH Asia 2025, December 15–18, 2025, Hong Kong, Hong Kong.

Our contributions can be summarized as follows:

We propose DeformSplat, a novel framework for rigidity- aware deformation of 3D Gaussians using only a single target image.

We present Gaussian-to-Pixel Matching strategy that con- nects the 3D Gaussian representation with the 2D target image to guide deformation.

WeproposeRigidPartSegmentationmethod,whichpreserves the original geometry by detecting rigid regions and con- straining their deformation.

Our method shows superior performance in single image Gaussian deformation and extends to applications such as frame interpolation and interactive manipulation.
rag_generated_news_article:
Headline:  
DeformSplat: Single-Image Deformation Estimation for 3D Gaussians Advances the Geometry Preservation Frontier

News Article:  
The task of estimating deformation in textureless plastic objects from a single RGB image stands as a persistent and formidable obstacle in computer vision and graphics. Conventional methods frequently demand multi-view or temporally continuous video data—a requirement fundamentally at odds with the practical constraints inherent to real-world acquisition, particularly in industrial inspection or minimally instrumented environments. The recent study on “Deformation Estimation of Textureless Objects from a Single Image” exemplifies this challenge, underscoring a pressing need for robust single-view solutions. It is in direct response to such scenarios that DeformSplat introduces a set of technically rigorous methodologies, promising a pathway to reconstructing geometrically consistent 3D deformations from a sole image input [Abstract, Real-World Anchor Event].

The core contribution of DeformSplat is its explicit targeting of the underconstrained single-image deformation regime. This regime, typified by occlusions, lack of texture, and the absence of supporting depth or camera pose information, severely complicates inference. To address these insufficiencies, DeformSplat leverages a two-pronged approach: Gaussian-to-Pixel Matching and Rigid Part Segmentation [Introduction, Abstract].

**Gaussian-to-Pixel Matching: Bridging 2D Observations and 3D Representations**  
Traditional pipelines for deformable reconstruction, especially those predicated on 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) or Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF), rely on explicit multi-view correspondence, yielding diminished efficacy or even outright failure when confronted with single-view constraints. DeformSplat’s Gaussian-to-Pixel Matching mechanism eradicates this key bottleneck: multi-view images are synthesized from the initial 3D Gaussian representation, and pixel-wise correspondences to the input image are then established using image matchers. The innovation lies in the selection of rendered viewpoints with maximized visual overlap, and the subsequent transfer of 2D-to-2D correspondences across domains—enabling direct Gaussian-to-Pixel mappings. This mapping is technically significant, as it forms a backbone for inference in the absence of extrinsic calibration or depth priors, and stands in contrast to previous approaches that either fail to preserve detailed geometry or exhibit a propensity for overfitting to sparse cues [Abstract, Introduction]. 

**Rigid Part Segmentation: Explicit Geometry Preservation in Deformable Reconstruction**  
The secondary component—Rigid Part Segmentation—addresses the equally critical challenge of maintaining geometric fidelity, especially in regions intended to remain rigid throughout deformation. DeformSplat initially groups Gaussians into candidate rigid clusters based on their pixel correspondences, then iteratively refines these clusters through a rigidity-aware optimization process. By regularizing the deformation process to treat rigid and non-rigid regions differentially, the method constrains potential overfitting while enabling flexible yet coherent shape adaptation [Introduction]. Unlike previous methods such as GESI, which have shown vulnerabilities in preserving fidelity under extended or complex deformations, DeformSplat’s explicit rigidity modeling supports high-fidelity structure retention [Introduction].

**Technical Significance and Comparative Performance**  
DeformSplat advances the state of the art in several central respects. Prior approaches leveraging diffusion models may enable some intuitive editing, but often at the expense of geometric consistency owing to the ambiguity and interpretative latitude of conditioning inputs. Moreover, direct geometry editing frameworks such as GESI, while capable of single-image-driven modifications, frequently distort fine structures under substantial shape changes. In extensive experiments, DeformSplat exhibits significantly improved deformation consistency and geometry preservation compared to both implicit and explicit precedent methodologies [Abstract]. Quantitative results, as referenced in the original study, demonstrate superior performance, particularly in the context of single-image 3D Gaussian deformation.

A particularly salient outcome of DeformSplat’s architectural design is its flexibility toward downstream applications. From high-quality frame interpolation—where consistent intermediate deformations are critical—to interactive object manipulation, the method demonstrates a capacity for generalization that addresses longstanding limitations imposed by input sparsity in traditional 3D reconstruction workflows [Abstract, Introduction].

**Conclusion and Perspective**  
The release of DeformSplat provides the computer vision and graphics research community with a verified, technically nuanced solution to the problem of single-view 3D deformation estimation, and establishes new benchmarks for both effectiveness and interpretability in this domain. Researchers with interests in photorealistic rendering, scene editing, or interactive modeling in minimal-data regimes should directly consult the originating paper and its supplementary resources to further evaluate the experimental methodology and resulting data.

Access the full paper and resources at the project page: https://vision3d-lab.github.io/deformsplat  
Citation: https://dx.doi.org/10.1145/3757377.3763937
